UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents a user's profile within the TaskFlow application, storing authentication-related details and linking to their data.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity.

email.description: The user's email address, used as a login credential and for email verification.

username.description: The user's chosen username, which can be used as an alternative login credential.

photoURL.description: The URL or Base64 data of the user's profile photo. Stored in Firestore to avoid Auth attribute length limits.

emailVerified.description: Indicates whether the user's email address has been successfully verified.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user profile was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user profile was last updated.

VerificationToken.title: VerificationToken
VerificationToken.description: Stores temporary tokens used for actions such as email verification, password reset, or linking social accounts.

id.description: Unique identifier for the VerificationToken entity.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile associated with this token. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N VerificationToken)

token.description: The unique, secret token string used for verification or resetting.

expiresAt.description: Timestamp indicating when this token expires and becomes invalid.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the verification token was generated.

OneTimePassword.title: OneTimePassword
OneTimePassword.description: Stores One-Time Passwords (OTPs) generated for phone number verification.

id.description: Unique identifier for the OneTimePassword entity.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile associated with this OTP. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N OneTimePassword)

phoneNumber.description: The phone number to which the OTP was sent, matching the user's registered phone number.

otpCode.description: The numeric One-Time Password code sent to the user's phone.

expiresAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the OTP expires and can no longer be used.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the OTP was generated.

ApplicationSetting.title: ApplicationSetting
ApplicationSetting.description: Stores global application-level settings that affect all users, such as the active authentication mode.

id.description: Unique identifier for the ApplicationSetting entity. This should be a fixed string (e.g., 'authenticationModeSetting') as it's intended to be a singleton entity.

authenticationMode.description: The current active authentication mode for the application. Can be 'localStorage' (default) or 'authenticate'.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when this application setting was last updated.

definition.description: Stores individual user profiles. The `userId` in the path corresponds to `request.auth.uid` for ownership-based access control. Access is restricted to the user whose `userId` matches the document ID.

Stores temporary tokens for email verification or password resets specific to a user. The `userId` in the path matches the `userId` field within the document for authorization independence. Access is restricted to the owner `userId` and tokens expire after a set time.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique ID of the user who owns this token.

params.name: tokenId
params.description: The unique ID of the verification token.

definition.description: Stores One-Time Passwords for phone number verification specific to a user. The `userId` in the path matches the `userId` field within the document for authorization independence. Access is restricted to the owner `userId` and OTPs expire quickly.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique ID of the user who requested this OTP.

params.name: otpId
params.description: The unique ID of the One-Time Password.

definition.description: Stores tasks created and managed by a specific user. The `userId` in the path corresponds to the `ownerId` of the task for strong ownership and authorization independence. Includes task status, repository, Azure ID, and denormalized 'prLinks' object (with dev, stage, prod, others arrays) for efficient access and clear schema.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique ID of the user who owns this task.

params.name: taskId
params.description: The unique ID of the task.

definition.description: Stores global application settings, specifically the active authentication mode. This is a singleton document with a fixed ID 'authenticationModeSetting'. Read access is granted to all authenticated users, while write access is restricted to administrators defined in the `/roles_admin` collection.

Collection to define users with administrative privileges using Database Access Control (DBAC). The existence of a document at `/roles_admin/{userId}` grants the admin role to that `userId`, used to secure write access to global settings like `ApplicationSetting`.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique ID of the user designated as an administrator.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure is designed following the core principles of Authorization Independence, Clarity of Intent, DBAC, and QAPs to ensure security, scalability, and debuggability for the TaskFlow application.

**Authorization Independence (CRITICAL):**
This design prioritizes eliminating `get()` calls in Firestore security rules, which are prone to breaking atomic operations and complicate debugging. This is achieved primarily through:
1.  **Path-Based Ownership:** For user-specific data such as `UserProfile`, `VerificationToken`, `OneTimePassword`, and `Task` entities, documents are nested under `/users/{userId}/`. In this structure, the `userId` is directly available in the path, allowing security rules to instantly verify `request.auth.uid == userId` without needing to fetch parent documents. This makes authorization checks atomic and highly efficient.
2.  **Denormalized Authorization Fields:** While path-based ownership provides strong authorization, key authorization fields (e.g., `userId` in `VerificationToken` and `OneTimePassword`, and `ownerId` in `Task`) are also explicitly stored within the document itself. This redundancy further enhances authorization independence, providing an additional layer of clarity and enabling simpler client-side validation, even if not strictly necessary for server-side rule enforcement when the path itself defines ownership.
3.  **Global Roles for DBAC:** For global settings like `ApplicationSetting`, write access is determined by checking the existence of a document in the `/roles_admin/{userId}` collection. This `exists()` check is an atomic operation and does not depend on data within the `ApplicationSetting` document itself, maintaining authorization independence.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters):**
The structure ensures that security rules do not act as data filters, guaranteeing efficient and secure `list` operations:
1.  **User-Scoped Collections:** Collections like `/users/{request.auth.uid}/tasks`, `/users/{request.auth.uid}/verificationTokens`, and `/users/{request.auth.uid}/oneTimePasswords` are inherently QAP-compliant. When a client performs a `list` query against `/users/{request.auth.uid}/tasks`, the security rules can directly enforce that `userId` in the path must match `request.auth.uid`. This ensures that a user can only ever retrieve their own tasks or tokens, preventing broad, inefficient, or insecure queries.
2.  **Singleton Global Settings:** The `ApplicationSetting` is modeled as a singleton document at `/appSettings/authenticationModeSetting`. Since there's only one document, `list` operations (if ever performed, typically `get` is used) are trivially QAP-compliant as there's no data to filter.
3.  **Explicit Role Collections:** The `/roles_admin/{userId}` collection uses document existence to grant roles. Access checks for administrative actions directly query this specific path (e.g., `exists(/databases/$(database)/documents/roles_admin/$(request.auth.uid))`), which is a direct access check, not a filtering operation.

**Structural Segregation & Access Modeling:**
Data is segregated based on its security posture:
*   **Private User Data:** All user-specific and sensitive data (`UserProfile`, `VerificationToken`, `OneTimePassword`, `Task`) is strictly private, nested under `/users/{userId}/` paths, aligning with the "Private Data" and "Hierarchical Paths for User-Owned Data" access models.
*   **Global Application Settings:** `ApplicationSetting` is a top-level collection (`/appSettings`) for global configuration, separating it from user-specific data. It's read-only for most users and writable by defined administrators.
*   **Role-Based Access Control (DBAC):** Admin roles are managed via the `/roles_admin/{userId}` collection, leveraging "Existence over Content" for clear and auditable role assignments.

**Derived Entities:**
Two entities, `Task` and `AdminRole`, are derived from the "App requirements" section as they were not explicitly provided in the `backend.entities` input. Their `$ref` values (`#/backend/entities/Task` and `#/backend/entities/AdminRole` respectively) refer to conceptual schemas that would be defined internally for application development.
